[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When fetching lists from GitHub API, use the --paginate flag to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).

[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/\${owner}/\${repo}/pulls/\${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO] Playwright MCP usage (browser automation via mcp__playwright__* tools).
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When you develop frontend web applications (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue, Angular, etc.), use Playwright MCP tools to test the UI in a real browser.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When WebFetch tool fails to retrieve expected content (e.g., returns empty content, JavaScript-rendered pages, or login-protected pages), use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for web browsing.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When WebSearch tool fails or returns insufficient results, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for internet search.

[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When you need to visually verify how a web page looks or take screenshots, use browser_take_screenshot from Playwright MCP.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When you need to fill forms, click buttons, or perform user interactions on web pages, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_click, browser_type, browser_fill_form).

[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When reproducing UI bugs, use browser_take_screenshot to capture the problem state before implementing any fix.
[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When fixing UI bugs, take before/after screenshots to provide visual evidence of the fix for human verification.

[2026-04-23T17:02:52.981Z] [INFO]    - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use the Read tool to view and analyze it. Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub "Not Found" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause "Could not process image" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows "HTML", "text", or "ASCII text", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing "github.com/user-attachments", retry with: curl -L -H "Authorization: token $(gh auth token)" -o <filename> "<url>" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.

[2026-04-23T17:03:18.985Z] [INFO]         "content": "1\t# Singh Anand (2006) - P≠NP Proof Attempt\n2\t\n3\t**Navigation:** [↑ Back to Repository Root](../../../README.md) | [All Proof Frameworks](../../../README.md#-formal-verification)\n4\t\n5\t**Attempt ID**: 27 (from Woeginger's list)\n6\t**Author**: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n7\t**Year**: 2006 (revised 2007)\n8\t**Claim**: P ≠ NP\n9\t**Status**: **Flawed** - Contains fundamental logical error\n10\t\n11\t---\n12\t\n13\t## Summary\n14\t\n15\tIn March 2006, Bhupinder Singh Anand published a paper claiming to prove P ≠ NP through an argument about non-standard models of Peano Arithmetic (PA). The paper argues:\n16\t\n17\t1. **If PA has no non-standard models, then P ≠ NP**\n18\t2. **PA has no non-standard models** (claimed proof via induction)\n19\t3. **Therefore, P ≠ NP**\n20\t\n21\t## Main Argument\n22\t\n23\tThe paper's core logical chain is:\n24\t\n25\t### Step 1: Connecting PA models to P vs NP\n26\t\n27\tThe author claims that:\n28\t- Gödel's undecidable formula R(x) is \"Turing-decidable\" (can be verified) but not provable in PA\n29\t- If every Turing-computable arithmetical tautology is PA-provable, then R(x) cannot be Turing-computable\n30\t- If PA has no non-standard models, then every Turing-computable tautology must be PA-provable\n31\t- Therefore: no non-standard models → P ≠ NP\n32\t\n33\t### Step 2: Claiming PA has no non-standard models\n34\t\n35\tThe author attempts to prove PA has no non-standard models by:\n36\t1. Defining formula G(x): `[x=0 ∨ ¬(∀y)¬(x=y')]` (\"x is 0 or x is a successor\")\n37\t2. Proving G(0) and G(x) → G(x') are PA-provable\n38\t3. Applying induction axiom to conclude (∀x)G(x) is PA-provable\n39\t4. Claiming this means every element is a natural number (no non-standard models)\n40\t\n41\t## The Fatal Error\n42\t\n43\tThe proof confuses **provability within PA** with **truth in all models of PA**. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of Gödel's completeness and incompleteness theorems.\n44\t\n45\t### Why the Argument Fails\n46\t\n47\t**Critical Mistake**: The author claims that proving `(∀x)G(x)` in PA eliminates non-standard models. However:\n48\t\n49\t1. **Gödel's Completeness Theorem** (1930) tells us: If a formula is provable in PA, then it is true in **all** models of PA (both standard and non-standard)\n50\t\n51\t2. **What `(∀x)G(x)` means**: This formula says \"every element is either 0 or a successor.\" This is indeed provable in PA, and it is true in **both** standard and non-standard models:\n52\t   - **Standard model**: Natural numbers {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} - clearly every element is 0 or a successor ✓\n53\t   - **Non-standard model**: Natural numbers + \"infinite\" elements - the infinite elements are also successors (of other infinite elements) ✓\n54\t\n55\t3. **The key insight**: In non-standard models, the formula G(x) is still true for every element, including the non-standard \"infinite\" elements. The non-standard elements are successors of other non-standard elements, forming infinite descending chains that don't violate the formula.\n56\t\n57\t4. **Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem** (1931) actually **proves** that PA has non-standard models (this is a consequence of the incompleteness theorem - PA cannot pin down the natural numbers uniquely)\n58\t\n59\t### The Deeper Issue\n60\t\n61\tThe argument fundamentally misunderstands the relationship between:\n62\t- **Syntactic provability** (what can be derived formally in PA)\n63\t- **Semantic truth** (what is true in models of PA)\n64\t- **Standard vs non-standard models**\n65\t\n66\tGödel's completeness theorem tells us these align: provable formulas are true in all models. But this doesn't mean we can use provability to eliminate non-standard models - quite the opposite! The incompleteness theorem guarantees non-standard models exist precisely because PA is incomplete.\n67\t\n68\t## Connection to P vs NP\n69\t\n70\tEven if the argument about non-standard models were correct (it isn't), the connection to P vs NP is problematic:\n71\t\n72\t1. **Conflates decidability concepts**: The paper confuses \"Turing-decidable\" (can verify solutions) with \"Turing-computable\" (can find solutions efficiently), but doesn't rigorously connect these to the P and NP complexity classes\n73\t\n74\t2. **No complexity analysis**: P vs NP is fundamentally about **computational complexity** (polynomial vs exponential time). The paper provides no analysis of time complexity or reductions between problems\n75\t\n76\t3. **Misapplies Gödel's theorem**: Gödel's incompleteness is about the **expressiveness** of formal systems, not about **computational complexity**\n77\t\n78\t## Source Documents\n79\t\n80\t- **ArXiv**: [math.GM/0603605](http://arxiv.org/abs/math.GM/0603605) (version 2, revised February 2007)\n81\t- **Woeginger's List**: [Entry #27](https://wscor.win.tue.nl/woeginger/P-versus-NP.htm)\n82\t\n83\t## Known Refutation\n84\t\n85\tThe proof is **not accepted by the mathematical community**. The fundamental error is well-known in mathematical logic:\n86\t\n87\t- **Well-established fact**: First-order Peano Arithmetic **does have non-standard models** (this follows from the Löwenheim-Skolem theorem and Gödel's incompleteness theorem)\n88\t- **Textbook result**: Every consistent first-order theory with an infinite model has models of all infinite cardinalities, including non-standard models\n89\t- **Counterexample**: The compactness theorem can be used to explicitly construct non-standard models of PA\n90\t\n91\t## Formalization Strategy\n92\t\n93\tOur formal verification exposes the error by:\n94\t\n95\t1. **Defining standard and non-standard models** of PA\n96\t2. **Formalizing the induction argument** that proves (∀x)G(x)\n97\t3. **Showing this formula holds in non-standard models too** - the critical oversight\n98\t4. **Demonstrating the formula cannot eliminate non-standard models**\n99\t5. **Referencing known results** that non-standard models exist\n100\t\n101\t## Lessons for P vs NP Research\n102\t\n103\tThis attempt illustrates several common pitfalls:\n104\t\n105\t1. **Complexity ≠ Logic**: Confusion between logical incompleteness and computational complexity\n106\t2. **Model theory is subtle**: Proving formulas in PA doesn't constrain which models exist\n107\t3. **Known barriers**: Any P vs NP proof must address relativization, natural proofs, and algebrization barriers - none mentioned here\n108\t4. **Requires complexity theory**: P vs NP needs explicit complexity analysis, not just logical arguments\n109\t\n110\t## Formal Verification\n111\t\n112\tWe provide formalizations in three proof assistants that:\n113\t- Encode the paper's argument\n114\t- Identify the step where the reasoning fails\n115\t- Demonstrate why non-standard models satisfy the formula\n116\t- Reference model theory results about PA\n117\t\n118\t### Implementations\n119\t\n120\t- `rocq/` - Rocq formalization\n121\t- `lean/` - Lean 4 formalization\n122\t- `isabelle/` - Isabelle/HOL formalization\n123\t\n124\t## References\n125\t\n126\t### The Attempt\n127\t- **Singh Anand, B.** (2006/2007). \"An elementary proof that P ≠ NP.\" *arXiv:math/0603605*\n128\t\n129\t### Relevant Model Theory\n130\t- **Gödel, K.** (1930). \"The completeness of the axioms of the functional calculus of logic.\" (Completeness theorem)\n131\t- **Gödel, K.** (1931). \"On formally undecidable propositions of Principia Mathematica and related systems I.\" (Incompleteness theorem)\n132\t- **Löwenheim, L.; Skolem, T.** Löwenheim-Skolem theorem (guarantees non-standard models)\n133\t- **Mendelson, E.** (1964). *Introduction to Mathematical Logic*. (Referenced in the paper)\n134\t\n135\t### P vs NP Background\n136\t- **Cook, S.** (1971). \"The complexity of theorem-proving procedures.\" *STOC*\n137\t- **Arora, S., Barak, B.** (2009). *Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach*\n138\t\n139\t## Status\n140\t\n141\t- ✅ Detailed analysis complete\n142\t- ✅ Error identified: Confusion between provability and model existence\n143\t- ✅ Rocq formalization: Complete\n144\t- ✅ Lean formalization: Complete\n145\t- ✅ Isabelle formalization: Complete\n146\t\n147\t---\n148\t\n149\t**Navigation:** [↑ Back to Repository Root](../../../README.md) | [P vs NP Task Description](../../../P_VS_NP_TASK_DESCRIPTION.md)\n150\t"

[2026-04-23T17:03:18.985Z] [INFO]       "filePath": "/tmp/gh-issue-solver-1776963751017/proofs/attempts/singh-anand-2006-pneqnp/README.md",
[2026-04-23T17:03:18.985Z] [INFO]       "content": "# Singh Anand (2006) - P≠NP Proof Attempt\n\n**Navigation:** [↑ Back to Repository Root](../../../README.md) | [All Proof Frameworks](../../../README.md#-formal-verification)\n\n**Attempt ID**: 27 (from Woeginger's list)\n**Author**: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n**Year**: 2006 (revised 2007)\n**Claim**: P ≠ NP\n**Status**: **Flawed** - Contains fundamental logical error\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\nIn March 2006, Bhupinder Singh Anand published a paper claiming to prove P ≠ NP through an argument about non-standard models of Peano Arithmetic (PA). The paper argues:\n\n1. **If PA has no non-standard models, then P ≠ NP**\n2. **PA has no non-standard models** (claimed proof via induction)\n3. **Therefore, P ≠ NP**\n\n## Main Argument\n\nThe paper's core logical chain is:\n\n### Step 1: Connecting PA models to P vs NP\n\nThe author claims that:\n- Gödel's undecidable formula R(x) is \"Turing-decidable\" (can be verified) but not provable in PA\n- If every Turing-computable arithmetical tautology is PA-provable, then R(x) cannot be Turing-computable\n- If PA has no non-standard models, then every Turing-computable tautology must be PA-provable\n- Therefore: no non-standard models → P ≠ NP\n\n### Step 2: Claiming PA has no non-standard models\n\nThe author attempts to prove PA has no non-standard models by:\n1. Defining formula G(x): `[x=0 ∨ ¬(∀y)¬(x=y')]` (\"x is 0 or x is a successor\")\n2. Proving G(0) and G(x) → G(x') are PA-provable\n3. Applying induction axiom to conclude (∀x)G(x) is PA-provable\n4. Claiming this means every element is a natural number (no non-standard models)\n\n## The Fatal Error\n\nThe proof confuses **provability within PA** with **truth in all models of PA**. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of Gödel's completeness and incompleteness theorems.\n\n### Why the Argument Fails\n\n**Critical Mistake**: The author claims that proving `(∀x)G(x)` in PA eliminates non-standard models. However:\n\n1. **Gödel's Completeness Theorem** (1930) tells us: If a formula is provable in PA, then it is true in **all** models of PA (both standard and non-standard)\n\n2. **What `(∀x)G(x)` means**: This formula says \"every element is either 0 or a successor.\" This is indeed provable in PA, and it is true in **both** standard and non-standard models:\n   - **Standard model**: Natural numbers {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} - clearly every element is 0 or a successor ✓\n   - **Non-standard model**: Natural numbers + \"infinite\" elements - the infinite elements are also successors (of other infinite elements) ✓\n\n3. **The key insight**: In non-standard models, the formula G(x) is still true for every element, including the non-standard \"infinite\" elements. The non-standard elements are successors of other non-standard elements, forming infinite descending chains that don't violate the formula.\n\n4. **Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem** (1931) actually **proves** that PA has non-standard models (this is a consequence of the incompleteness theorem - PA cannot pin down the natural numbers uniquely)\n\n### The Deeper Issue\n\nThe argument fundamentally misunderstands the relationship between:\n- **Syntactic provability** (what can be derived formally in PA)\n- **Semantic truth** (what is true in models of PA)\n- **Standard vs non-standard models**\n\nGödel's completeness theorem tells us these align: provable formulas are true in all models. But this doesn't mean we can use provability to eliminate non-standard models - quite the opposite! The incompleteness theorem guarantees non-standard models exist precisely because PA is incomplete.\n\n## Connection to P vs NP\n\nEven if the argument about non-standard models were correct (it isn't), the connection to P vs NP is problematic:\n\n1. **Conflates decidability concepts**: The paper confuses \"Turing-decidable\" (can verify solutions) with \"Turing-computable\" (can find solutions efficiently), but doesn't rigorously connect these to the P and NP complexity classes\n\n2. **No complexity analysis**: P vs NP is fundamentally about **computational complexity** (polynomial vs exponential time). The paper provides no analysis of time complexity or reductions between problems\n\n3. **Misapplies Gödel's theorem**: Gödel's incompleteness is about the **expressiveness** of formal systems, not about **computational complexity**\n\n## Source Documents\n\n- **ArXiv**: [math.GM/0603605](http://arxiv.org/abs/math.GM/0603605) (version 2, revised February 2007)\n- **Woeginger's List**: [Entry #27](https://wscor.win.tue.nl/woeginger/P-versus-NP.htm)\n\n## Known Refutation\n\nThe proof is **not accepted by the mathematical community**. The fundamental error is well-known in mathematical logic:\n\n- **Well-established fact**: First-order Peano Arithmetic **does have non-standard models** (this follows from the Löwenheim-Skolem theorem and Gödel's incompleteness theorem)\n- **Textbook result**: Every consistent first-order theory with an infinite model has models of all infinite cardinalities, including non-standard models\n- **Counterexample**: The compactness theorem can be used to explicitly construct non-standard models of PA\n\n## Formalization Strategy\n\nOur formal verification exposes the error by:\n\n1. **Defining standard and non-standard models** of PA\n2. **Formalizing the induction argument** that proves (∀x)G(x)\n3. **Showing this formula holds in non-standard models too** - the critical oversight\n4. **Demonstrating the formula cannot eliminate non-standard models**\n5. **Referencing known results** that non-standard models exist\n\n## Lessons for P vs NP Research\n\nThis attempt illustrates several common pitfalls:\n\n1. **Complexity ≠ Logic**: Confusion between logical incompleteness and computational complexity\n2. **Model theory is subtle**: Proving formulas in PA doesn't constrain which models exist\n3. **Known barriers**: Any P vs NP proof must address relativization, natural proofs, and algebrization barriers - none mentioned here\n4. **Requires complexity theory**: P vs NP needs explicit complexity analysis, not just logical arguments\n\n## Formal Verification\n\nWe provide formalizations in three proof assistants that:\n- Encode the paper's argument\n- Identify the step where the reasoning fails\n- Demonstrate why non-standard models satisfy the formula\n- Reference model theory results about PA\n\n### Implementations\n\n- `rocq/` - Rocq formalization\n- `lean/` - Lean 4 formalization\n- `isabelle/` - Isabelle/HOL formalization\n\n## References\n\n### The Attempt\n- **Singh Anand, B.** (2006/2007). \"An elementary proof that P ≠ NP.\" *arXiv:math/0603605*\n\n### Relevant Model Theory\n- **Gödel, K.** (1930). \"The completeness of the axioms of the functional calculus of logic.\" (Completeness theorem)\n- **Gödel, K.** (1931). \"On formally undecidable propositions of Principia Mathematica and related systems I.\" (Incompleteness theorem)\n- **Löwenheim, L.; Skolem, T.** Löwenheim-Skolem theorem (guarantees non-standard models)\n- **Mendelson, E.** (1964). *Introduction to Mathematical Logic*. (Referenced in the paper)\n\n### P vs NP Background\n- **Cook, S.** (1971). \"The complexity of theorem-proving procedures.\" *STOC*\n- **Arora, S., Barak, B.** (2009). *Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach*\n\n## Status\n\n- ✅ Detailed analysis complete\n- ✅ Error identified: Confusion between provability and model existence\n- ✅ Rocq formalization: Complete\n- ✅ Lean formalization: Complete\n- ✅ Isabelle formalization: Complete\n\n---\n\n**Navigation:** [↑ Back to Repository Root](../../../README.md) | [P vs NP Task Description](../../../P_VS_NP_TASK_DESCRIPTION.md)\n",

antecedent is false, giving us no information about P vs NP -/\n141\ttheorem singh_anand_proves_nothing_about_P_vs_NP :\n142\t    ∀ (conclusion : Prop),\n143\t    ¬PA_has_no_nonstandard_models →\n144\t    (PA_has_no_nonstandard_models → conclusion) →\n145\t    True := by\n146\t  intros _ _ _\n147\t  trivial\n148\t\n149\t-- Detailed Error Analysis\n150\t\n151\t/-- The key misunderstanding: provability vs model elimination -/\n152\tstructure ErrorAnalysis where\n153\t  /-- Error 1: Confusing truth in all models with elimination of models -/\n154\t  error_1 : String := \"Provable formulas are true in ALL models, not just standard ones\"\n155\t\n156\t  /-- Error 2: Contradicts known mathematical results -/\n157\t  error_2 : String := \"PA provably has non-standard models (Gödel, Löwenheim-Skolem)\"\n158\t\n159\t  /-- Error 3: Weak connection to complexity theory -/\n160\t  error_3 : String := \"No rigorous analysis of polynomial time or NP-completeness\"\n161\t\n162\t/-- The formalized error analysis -/\n163\tdef singhAnandErrors : ErrorAnalysis := {}\n164\t\n165\t/-\n166\t  SUMMARY OF ERRORS IN SINGH ANAND'S PROOF:\n167\t\n168\t  1. MAIN ERROR: Confuses provability in PA with eliminating non-standard models\n169\t     - Provable formulas are true in ALL models (including non-standard ones)\n170\t     - The formula ∀x.G(x) holds in non-standard models too\n171\t\n172\t  2. CONTRADICTS KNOWN RESULTS: First-order PA provably has non-standard models\n173\t     - Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem\n174\t     - Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem\n175\t     - Compactness Theorem\n176\t\n177\t  3. WEAK CONNECTION TO P vs NP: Even if PA had no non-standard models,\n178\t     the connection to computational complexity is not rigorous\n179\t     - No analysis of polynomial time\n180\t     - No discussion of NP-completeness\n181\t     - Confuses logical decidability with computational complexity\n182\t\n183\t  STATUS: This proof attempt is fundamentally flawed and does not\n184\t          prove P ≠ NP.\n185\t-/\n186\t"

[2026-04-23T17:03:24.124Z] [INFO]       "content": "/-\n  Singh Anand (2006) - P≠NP Proof Attempt Formalization\n\n  This file formalizes and identifies the error in Singh Anand's 2006\n  attempt to prove P ≠ NP through an argument about non-standard models\n  of Peano Arithmetic.\n\n  Reference: arXiv:math/0603605v2\n  Status: FLAWED - Contains fundamental model-theoretic error\n-/\n\n/-- Abstract representation of models of Peano Arithmetic -/\nstructure Model where\n  domain : Type\n  zero : domain\n  succ : domain → domain\n\n/-- The standard model is the natural numbers -/\ndef StandardModel : Model := {\n  domain := Nat\n  zero := 0\n  succ := Nat.succ\n}\n\n/-- Non-standard models exist (axiomatized for this formalization) -/\naxiom NonStandardModel : Model\n\n/-- Non-standard model differs from standard model -/\naxiom nonstandard_differs : NonStandardModel ≠ StandardModel\n\n-- PA Formulas and Provability\n\n/-- Abstract representation of PA formulas -/\naxiom PAFormula : Type\n\n/-- Provability relation in PA -/\naxiom provableInPA : PAFormula → Prop\n\n/-- Model satisfaction relation -/\naxiom satisfies : Model → PAFormula → Prop\n\n/-- The formula G(x): \"x = 0 or x is a successor\" -/\naxiom formulaG : PAFormula\n\n/-- The universal quantification ∀x.G(x) -/\naxiom forallG : PAFormula\n\n-- Gödel's Completeness Theorem\n\n/-- If a formula is provable in PA, it holds in ALL models -/\naxiom goedel_completeness :\n  ∀ (φ : PAFormula), provableInPA φ → ∀ (M : Model), satisfies M φ\n\n-- Key Provability Facts from PA\n\n/-- The formula ∀x.G(x) is provable via induction -/\naxiom provable_forallG : provableInPA forallG\n\n-- Singh Anand's Argument (FORMALIZED)\n\n/-- Step 1: (∀x)G(x) is provable in PA -/\ntheorem singh_anand_step1 : provableInPA forallG :=\n  provable_forallG\n\n/-- Step 2: By completeness, (∀x)G(x) holds in all models -/\ntheorem singh_anand_step2 : ∀ (M : Model), satisfies M forallG := by\n  intro M\n  exact goedel_completeness forallG provable_forallG M\n\n-- Step 3: THE ERROR - Singh Anand claims this eliminates non-standard models\n\n/-- Singh Anand's incorrect claim -/\naxiom singh_anand_incorrect_claim :\n  provableInPA forallG → ¬∃ (M : Model), M ≠ StandardModel\n\n-- REFUTATION: The Error Exposed\n\n/-- Theorem: Singh Anand's claim leads to contradiction -/\ntheorem singh_anand_argument_is_wrong : False := by\n  -- We have a non-standard model\n  have h_nonstandard_exists : ∃ M : Model, M ≠ StandardModel := by\n    exact ⟨NonStandardModel, nonstandard_differs⟩\n\n  -- Singh Anand claims this is impossible\n  have h_singh_claim : ¬∃ (M : Model), M ≠ StandardModel := by\n    exact singh_anand_incorrect_claim provable_forallG\n\n  -- Contradiction!\n  exact h_singh_claim h_nonstandard_exists\n\n-- The Core Mistake Explained\n\n/-\n  CRITICAL ERROR: Singh Anand confuses \"provable in PA\" with\n  \"eliminates non-standard models\"\n\n  FACT 1 (Gödel's Completeness):\n    Provable formulas are true in ALL models (standard AND non-standard)\n\n  FACT 2 (What G(x) really says):\n    In the standard model: Every nat is 0 or a successor of a nat ✓\n    In non-standard models: Every element (including \"infinite\" ones)\n                            is 0 or a successor of something ✓\n\n  The non-standard elements are successors of OTHER non-standard elements!\n  The formula G(x) is satisfied in non-standard models too.\n\n  FACT 3 (Gödel's Incompleteness + Löwenheim-Skolem):\n    First-order PA MUST have non-standard models. This is a deep theorem.\n-/\n\n-- The Formula Works in Non-Standard Models\n\n/-- The formula ∀x.G(x) is satisfied even in non-standard models -/\ntheorem G_holds_in_nonstandard_model : satisfies NonStandardModel forallG := by\n  exact singh_anand_step2 NonStandardModel\n\n/-- This demonstrates the error: proving G doesn't eliminate non-standard models -/\ntheorem proving_G_does_not_eliminate_nonstandard_models :\n    provableInPA forallG ∧ (∃ M : Model, M ≠ StandardModel) := by\n  constructor\n  · exact provable_forallG\n  · exact ⟨NonStandardModel, nonstandard_differs⟩\n\n-- Connection to P vs NP\n\n/-- P vs NP proposition -/\naxiom P_equals_NP : Prop\n\n/-- Claim about PA models -/\naxiom PA_has_no_nonstandard_models : Prop\n\n/-- Singh Anand's claimed implication -/\naxiom singh_anand_main_claim :\n  PA_has_no_nonstandard_models → ¬P_equals_NP\n\n/-- But PA having non-standard models is actually TRUE -/\naxiom PA_has_nonstandard_models_TRUE : ¬PA_has_no_nonstandard_models\n\n/-- So the implication's antecedent is false, giving us no information about P vs NP -/\ntheorem singh_anand_proves_nothing_about_P_vs_NP :\n    ∀ (conclusion : Prop),\n    ¬PA_has_no_nonstandard_models →\n    (PA_has_no_nonstandard_models → conclusion) →\n    True := by\n  intros _ _ _\n  trivial\n\n-- Detailed Error Analysis\n\n/-- The key misunderstanding: provability vs model elimination -/\nstructure ErrorAnalysis where\n  /-- Error 1: Confusing truth in all models with elimination of models -/\n  error_1 : String := \"Provable formulas are true in ALL models, not just standard ones\"\n\n  /-- Error 2: Contradicts known mathematical results -/\n  error_2 : String := \"PA provably has non-standard models (Gödel, Löwenheim-Skolem)\"\n\n  /-- Error 3: Weak connection to complexity theory -/\n  error_3 : String := \"No rigorous analysis of polynomial time or NP-completeness\"\n\n/-- The formalized error analysis -/\ndef singhAnandErrors : ErrorAnalysis := {}\n\n/-\n  SUMMARY OF ERRORS IN SINGH ANAND'S PROOF:\n\n  1. MAIN ERROR: Confuses provability in PA with eliminating non-standard models\n     - Provable formulas are true in ALL models (including non-standard ones)\n     - The formula ∀x.G(x) holds in non-standard models too\n\n  2. CONTRADICTS KNOWN RESULTS: First-order PA provably has non-standard models\n     - Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem\n     - Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem\n     - Compactness Theorem\n\n  3. WEAK CONNECTION TO P vs NP: Even if PA had no non-standard models,\n     the connection to computational complexity is not rigorous\n     - No analysis of polynomial time\n     - No discussion of NP-completeness\n     - Confuses logical decidability with computational complexity\n\n  STATUS: This proof attempt is fundamentally flawed and does not\n          prove P ≠ NP.\n-/\n",

*)\n15\t\n16\t(** We model PA and its interpretations abstractly *)\n17\t\n18\t(** A model is a domain with interpretations of PA symbols *)\n19\tRecord Model : Type := mkModel {\n20\t  domain : Type;\n21\t  zero : domain;\n22\t  succ : domain -> domain;\n23\t  (* We abstract away full PA structure for simplicity *)\n24\t}.\n25\t\n26\t(** Standard model: natural numbers *)\n27\tDefinition StandardModel : Model :=\n28\t  mkModel nat 0 S.\n29\t\n30\t(** Non-standard models exist (we axiomatize their existence) *)\n31\tAxiom NonStandardModel : Model.\n32\tAxiom NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard :\n33\t  NonStandardModel <> StandardModel.\n34\t\n35\t(** * PA Formula and Provability *)\n36\t\n37\t(** Abstract representation of PA formulas *)\n38\tParameter PAFormula : Type.\n39\tParameter provable_in_PA : PAFormula -> Prop.\n40\t\n41\t(** The formula G(x): \"x = 0 or x is a successor\" *)\n42\tParameter formula_G : PAFormula.\n43\t\n44\t(** Model satisfaction relation *)\n45\tParameter satisfies : forall (M : Model), PAFormula -> Prop.\n46\t\n47\t(** * Gödel's Completeness Theorem *)\n48\t\n49\t(** If a formula is provable in PA, it holds in ALL models *)\n50\tAxiom Goedel_completeness :\n51\t  forall (phi : PAFormula),\n52\t    provable_in_PA phi ->\n53\t    forall (M : Model), satisfies M phi.\n54\t\n55\t(** * Key Axioms from PA *)\n56\t\n57\t(** G(0) is provable *)\n58\tAxiom G_base : provable_in_PA formula_G.\n59\t\n60\t(** The induction axiom leads to proving ∀x.G(x) *)\n61\tParameter forall_G : PAFormula.\n62\tAxiom provable_forall_G : provable_in_PA forall_G.\n63\t\n64\t(** * Singh Anand's Argument (FORMALIZED) *)\n65\t\n66\t(** Step 1: Since (∀x)G(x) is provable in PA... *)\n67\tTheorem singh_anand_step1 : provable_in_PA forall_G.\n68\tProof.\n69\t  apply provable_forall_G.\n70\tQed.\n71\t\n72\t(** Step 2: By completeness, it holds in all models *)\n73\tTheorem singh_anand_step2 :\n74\t  forall (M : Model), satisfies M forall_G.\n75\tProof.\n76\t  intro M.\n77\t  apply Goedel_completeness.\n78\t  apply singh_anand_step1.\n79\tQed.\n80\t\n81\t(** Step 3: THE ERROR - Singh Anand claims this eliminates non-standard models *)\n82\t\n83\t(** Singh Anand's incorrect conclusion *)\n84\tAxiom singh_anand_incorrect_claim :\n85\t  provable_in_PA forall_G ->\n86\t  ~exists (M : Model), M <> StandardModel.\n87\t\n88\t(** * REFUTATION: The Error Exposed *)\n89\t\n90\t(** Theorem: Singh Anand's claim leads to contradiction *)\n91\tTheorem singh_anand_argument_is_wrong : False.\n92\tProof.\n93\t  (** We have a non-standard model *)\n94\t  assert (H_nonstandard_exists : exists M : Model, M <> StandardModel).\n95\t  { exists NonStandardModel. apply NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard. }\n96\t\n97\t  (** Singh Anand claims this is impossible *)\n98\t  assert (H_singh_claim : ~exists (M : Model), M <> StandardModel).\n99\t  { apply singh_anand_incorrect_claim. apply provable_forall_G. }\n100\t\n101\t  (** Contradiction! *)\n102\t  contradiction.\n103\tQed.\n104\t\n105\t(** * The Core Mistake Explained *)\n106\t\n107\t(**\n108\t   CRITICAL ERROR: Singh Anand confuses \"provable in PA\" with\n109\t   \"eliminates non-standard models\"\n110\t\n111\t   FACT 1 (Gödel's Completeness):\n112\t     Provable formulas are true in ALL models (standard AND non-standard)\n113\t\n114\t   FACT 2 (What G(x) really says):\n115\t     In the standard model: Every nat is 0 or a successor of a nat ✓\n116\t     In non-standard models: Every element (including \"infinite\" ones)\n117\t                             is 0 or a successor of something ✓\n118\t\n119\t   The non-standard elements are successors of OTHER non-standard elements!\n120\t   The formula G(x) is satisfied in non-standard models too.\n121\t\n122\t   FACT 3 (Gödel's Incompleteness + Löwenheim-Skolem):\n123\t     First-order PA MUST have non-standard models. This is a deep theorem.\n124\t*)\n125\t\n126\t(** * The Formula Works in Non-Standard Models *)\n127\t\n128\t(** The formula ∀x.G(x) is satisfied even in non-standard models *)\n129\tTheorem G_holds_in_nonstandard_model :\n130\t  satisfies NonStandardModel forall_G.\n131\tProof.\n132\t  apply singh_anand_step2.\n133\tQed.\n134\t\n135\t(** This demonstrates the error: proving G doesn't eliminate non-standard models *)\n136\tCorollary proving_G_does_not_eliminate_nonstandard_models :\n137\t  provable_in_PA forall_G /\\ (exists M : Model, M <> StandardModel).\n138\tProof.\n139\t  split.\n140\t  - apply provable_forall_G.\n141\t  - exists NonStandardModel. apply NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard.\n142\tQed.\n143\t\n144\t(** * Connection to P vs NP *)\n145\t\n146\t(** Even if the model theory were correct, the connection to P≠NP is unfounded *)\n147\t\n148\tParameter P_equals_NP : Prop.\n149\tParameter PA_has_no_nonstandard_models : Prop.\n150\t\n151\t(** Singh Anand's claimed implication *)\n152\tAxiom singh_anand_main_claim :\n153\t  PA_has_no_nonstandard_models -> ~P_equals_NP.\n154\t\n155\t(** But we've shown PA has non-standard models is actually TRUE *)\n156\tAxiom PA_has_nonstandard_models_TRUE :\n157\t  ~PA_has_no_nonstandard_models.\n158\t\n159\t(** So the implication's antecedent is false, giving us no information about P vs NP *)\n160\tTheorem singh_anand_proves_nothing_about_P_vs_NP :\n161\t  forall (conclusion_about_PvsNP : Prop),\n162\t    ~PA_has_no_nonstandard_models ->\n163\t    (PA_has_no_nonstandard_models -> conclusion_about_PvsNP) ->\n164\t    True.  (* We learn nothing *)\n165\tProof.\n166\t  intros conclusion H_false_premise H_implication.\n167\t  trivial.\n168\tQed.\n169\t\n170\t(** * Summary *)\n171\t\n172\t(**\n173\t   ERRORS IN SINGH ANAND'S PROOF:\n174\t\n175\t   1. MAIN ERROR: Confuses provability in PA with eliminating non-standard models\n176\t      - Provable formulas are true in ALL models (including non-standard ones)\n177\t      - The formula ∀x.G(x) holds in non-standard models too\n178\t\n179\t   2. CONTRADICTS KNOWN RESULTS: First-order PA provably has non-standard models\n180\t      - Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem\n181\t      - Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem\n182\t      - Compactness Theorem\n183\t\n184\t   3. to prove P ≠ NP through an argument about non-standard models\n    of Peano Arithmetic.\n\n    Reference: arXiv:math/0603605v2\n    Status: FLAWED - Contains fundamental model-theoretic error\n*)\n\nFrom Stdlib Require Import Classical_Prop.\nFrom Stdlib Require Import Ensembles.\n\n(** * Abstract Model Theory Framework *)\n\n(** We model PA and its interpretations abstractly *)\n\n(** A model is a domain with interpretations of PA symbols *)\nRecord Model : Type := mkModel {\n  domain : Type;\n  zero : domain;\n  succ : domain -> domain;\n  (* We abstract away full PA structure for simplicity *)\n}.\n\n(** Standard model: natural numbers *)\nDefinition StandardModel : Model :=\n  mkModel nat 0 S.\n\n(** Non-standard models exist (we axiomatize their existence) *)\nAxiom NonStandardModel : Model.\nAxiom NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard :\n  NonStandardModel <> StandardModel.\n\n(** * PA Formula and Provability *)\n\n(** Abstract representation of PA formulas *)\nParameter PAFormula : Type.\nParameter provable_in_PA : PAFormula -> Prop.\n\n(** The formula G(x): \"x = 0 or x is a successor\" *)\nParameter formula_G : PAFormula.\n\n(** Model satisfaction relation *)\nParameter satisfies : forall (M : Model), PAFormula -> Prop.\n\n(** * Gödel's Completeness Theorem *)\n\n(** If a formula is provable in PA, it holds in ALL models *)\nAxiom Goedel_completeness :\n  forall (phi : PAFormula),\n    provable_in_PA phi ->\n    forall (M : Model), satisfies M phi.\n\n(** * Key Axioms from PA *)\n\n(** G(0) is provable *)\nAxiom G_base : provable_in_PA formula_G.\n\n(** The induction axiom leads to proving ∀x.G(x) *)\nParameter forall_G : PAFormula.\nAxiom provable_forall_G : provable_in_PA forall_G.\n\n(** * Singh Anand's Argument (FORMALIZED) *)\n\n(** Step 1: Since (∀x)G(x) is provable in PA... *)\nTheorem singh_anand_step1 : provable_in_PA forall_G.\nProof.\n  apply provable_forall_G.\nQed.\n\n(** Step 2: By completeness, it holds in all models *)\nTheorem singh_anand_step2 :\n  forall (M : Model), satisfies M forall_G.\nProof.\n  intro M.\n  apply Goedel_completeness.\n  apply singh_anand_step1.\nQed.\n\n(** Step 3: THE ERROR - Singh Anand claims this eliminates non-standard models *)\n\n(** Singh Anand's incorrect conclusion *)\nAxiom singh_anand_incorrect_claim :\n  provable_in_PA forall_G ->\n  ~exists (M : Model), M <> StandardModel.\n\n(** * REFUTATION: The Error Exposed *)\n\n(** Theorem: Singh Anand's claim leads to contradiction *)\nTheorem singh_anand_argument_is_wrong : False.\nProof.\n  (** We have a non-standard model *)\n  assert (H_nonstandard_exists : exists M : Model, M <> StandardModel).\n  { exists NonStandardModel. apply NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard. }\n\n  (** Singh Anand claims this is impossible *)\n  assert (H_singh_claim : ~exists (M : Model), M <> StandardModel).\n  { apply singh_anand_incorrect_claim. apply provable_forall_G. }\n\n  (** Contradiction! *)\n  contradiction.\nQed.\n\n(** * The Core Mistake Explained *)\n\n(**\n   CRITICAL ERROR: Singh Anand confuses \"provable in PA\" with\n   \"eliminates non-standard models\"\n\n   FACT 1 (Gödel's Completeness):\n     Provable formulas are true in ALL models (standard AND non-standard)\n\n   FACT 2 (What G(x) really says):\n     In the standard model: Every nat is 0 or a successor of a nat ✓\n     In non-standard models: Every element (including \"infinite\" ones)\n                             is 0 or a successor of something ✓\n\n   The non-standard elements are successors of OTHER non-standard elements!\n   The formula G(x) is satisfied in non-standard models too.\n\n   FACT 3 (Gödel's Incompleteness + Löwenheim-Skolem):\n     First-order PA MUST have non-standard models. This is a deep theorem.\n*)\n\n(** * The Formula Works in Non-Standard Models *)\n\n(** The formula ∀x.G(x) is satisfied even in non-standard models *)\nTheorem G_holds_in_nonstandard_model :\n  satisfies NonStandardModel forall_G.\nProof.\n  apply singh_anand_step2.\nQed.\n\n(** This demonstrates the error: proving G doesn't eliminate non-standard models *)\nCorollary proving_G_does_not_eliminate_nonstandard_models :\n  provable_in_PA forall_G /\\ (exists M : Model, M <> StandardModel).\nProof.\n  split.\n  - apply provable_forall_G.\n  - exists NonStandardModel. apply NonStandardModel_differs_from_standard.\nQed.\n\n(** * Connection to P vs NP *)\n\n(** Even if the model theory were correct, the connection to P≠NP is unfounded *)\n\nParameter P_equals_NP : Prop.\nParameter PA_has_no_nonstandard_models : Prop.\n\n(** Singh Anand's claimed implication *)\nAxiom singh_anand_main_claim :\n  PA_has_no_nonstandard_models -> ~P_equals_NP.\n\n(** But we've shown PA has non-standard models is actually TRUE *)\nAxiom PA_has_nonstandard_models_TRUE :\n  ~PA_has_no_nonstandard_models.\n\n(** So the implication's antecedent is false, giving us no information about P vs NP *)\nTheorem singh_anand_proves_nothing_about_P_vs_NP :\n  forall (conclusion_about_PvsNP : Prop),\n    ~PA_has_no_nonstandard_models ->\n    (PA_has_no_nonstandard_models -> conclusion_about_PvsNP) ->\n    True.  (* We learn nothing *)\nProof.\n  intros conclusion H_false_premise H_implication.\n  trivial.\nQed.\n\n(** * Summary *)\n\n(**\n   ERRORS IN SINGH ANAND'S PROOF:\n\n   1. MAIN ERROR: Confuses provability in PA with eliminating non-standard models\n      - Provable formulas are true in ALL models (including non-standard ones)\n      - The formula ∀x.G(x) holds in non-standard models too\n\n   2. CONTRADICTS KNOWN RESULTS: First-order PA provably has non-standard models\n      - Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem\n      - Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem\n      - Compactness Theorem\n\n   3. WEAK CONNECTION TO P vs NP: Even if PA had no non-standard models,\n      the connection to computational complexity is not rigorous\n      - No analysis of polynomial time\n      - No discussion of NP-completeness\n      - Confuses logical decidability with computational complexity\n\n   STATUS: This proof attempt is fundamentally flawed and does not\n           prove P ≠ NP.\n*)\n",

Time**: The algorithm runs in at most O(n⁴) time, which is polynomial\n21\t5. **P=NP Conclusion**: Since ATSP is NP-hard, solving it in polynomial time would prove P = NP\n22\t\n23\t### Key Technical between every pair of vertices\n42\t- **TSP** finds the shortest path that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start\n43\t- These are fundamentally different problems with different constraints\n44\t\n45\t### The Critical Distinction\n46\t\n47\t#### What Floyd-Warshall Does:\n48\t- Input: Weighted graph\n49\t- Output: Shortest distance between every pair of vertices\n50\t- Constraint: Find the minimum-weight path (may reuse vertices and edges)"

[2026-04-23T17:03:33.821Z] [INFO]       "content": "# Howard Kleiman (2006) - P=NP via Modified Floyd-Warshall Algorithm for ATSP\n\n**Attempt ID**: 37 (from Woeginger's list)\n**Author**: Howard Kleiman\n**Year**: 2006 (December)\n**Claim**: P = NP\n**Status**: Refuted\n\n## Summary\n\nIn December 2006, Howard Kleiman proposed a polynomial-time algorithm for the Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP) using a modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm. The argument was that if ATSP (an NP-hard problem) can be solved in polynomial time, this would prove P = NP.\n\n## Main Argument\n\n### The Approach\n\n1. **Matrix Transformation**: Building on work by Jonker and Volgenannt, transform an n×n asymmetric cost matrix M(a) into a 2n×2n symmetric cost matrix M(s) with special structural properties\n2. **Modified Floyd-Warshall**: Apply a modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm to the symmetric matrix M(s)\n3. **Tour Extraction**: Extract an optimal tour in M(a) from the optimal tour in M(s)\n4. **Polynomial Time**: The algorithm runs in at most O(n⁴) time, which is polynomial\n5. **P=NP Conclusion**: Since ATSP is NP-hard, solving it in polynomial time would prove P = NP\n\n### Key Technical Claim\n\nThe crucial claim was that:\n- The transformation from M(a) to M(s) preserves optimality: \"an optimal tour in M(s) yields an optimal tour in M(a)\"\n- The modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm can find an optimal tour in M(s) in polynomial time\n- Therefore, we can solve ATSP in polynomial time\n\n### Stated Complexity\n\nThe paper claims a time complexity of **O(n⁴)**, which is polynomial in the size of the problem.\n\n## The Error\n\n### Fundamental Flaw: Floyd-Warshall Solves a Different Problem\n\n**The Error**: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm solves the **all-pairs shortest path problem**, not the **Traveling Salesman Problem**.\n\n**Why This Matters**:\n- **Floyd-Warshall** finds the shortest path between every pair of vertices\n- **TSP** finds the shortest path that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start\n- These are fundamentally different problems with different constraints\n\n### The Critical Distinction\n\n#### What Floyd-Warshall Does:\n- Input: Weighted graph\n- Output: Shortest distance between every pair of vertices\n- Constraint: Find the minimum-weight path (may reuse vertices and edges)",

[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO] {
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]   "type": "user",
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]   "message": {
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]     "role": "user",
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]     "content": [
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]       {
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]         "tool_use_id": "toolu_01E9L9MwuqBXVkybYLcwydCy",
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]         "type": "tool_result",
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Original Paper: The Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem\n\n**Author:** Howard Kleiman\n**Year:** 2006 (submitted December 5, 2006; revised December 9, 2006)\n**arXiv ID:** [math.CO/0612114](https://arxiv.org/abs/math.CO/0612114)\n**Subject Classification:** General Mathematics (math.GM); MSC: 05-XX\n**Woeginger's List:** Entry #37\n\n---\n\n## Abstract\n\nThe paper proposes a polynomial-time algorithm for solving the Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP) by building on work by Jonker and Volgenannt. The approach transforms an n×n asymmetric cost matrix into a 2n×2n symmetric matrix with special properties, then applies a modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find an optimal tour.\n\nThe author acknowledges: \"If the proof of theorem 1 in section II is correct, since the asymmetric traveling salesman problem is NP-hard, P would equal NP.\"\n\n---\n\n## Paper Summary\n\n### I. Introduction\n\nStarting with M(a), an n×n asymmetric cost matrix, Jonker and Volgenannt [1] transformed it into a 2n × 2n symmetric cost matrix, M(s), where M(s) has unusual properties. One such property is that an optimal tour in M(s) yields an optimal tour in M(a).\n\nModifying M(s), the paper applies the modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm given in [2] to M(s). Let T be a tour that is an upper bound for an optimal tour in M(a). Due to the structure of M(s), the claim is that we either can always obtain an optimal tour in M(s) that is derived from only one minimal positively-valued cycle in σ_T^(-1)M whose value is less than |T| (i.e., we don't have to link circuits), or else T = T_OPT. Thus, the paper claims we can obtain an optimal tour in M(a) in at most polynomial running time.\n\n**Critical Statement:** \"If the proof of theorem 1 in section II is correct, since the asymmetric traveling salesman problem is NP-hard, P would equal NP.\"\n\n### II. A Theorem\n\n**Theorem 1.**\n\nLet M(a) be an n×n asymmetric cost matrix where a_ii = ∞, i = 1,2,...,n. Using a modified version of the symmetric cost matrix, M(s), obtained by Jonker and Volgenannt [1] as well as a result of Kleiman in [2] and the use of the modified F-W algorithm, we prove that we always can obtain an optimal solution to M(a) in polynomial time.\n\n**Proof Outline:**\n\n1. **Normalization:** If M(a) contains a non-positive entry, let m be the smallest value of all the entries in M(a). Add -m+1 to each entry of M(a) so that each entry now has a positive value.\n\n2. **Matrix Construction:**\n   - Let M_∞ be an n×n matrix each of whose entries is ∞\n   - Change each diagonal entry of M(a) into 0 to obtain the matrix M(a)_d\n   - Define M(a)_d^T as the transpose of M(a)_d\n   - Define the 2n × 2n symmetric matrix as:\n     ```\n     M(s) = [ M_∞        M(a)_d   ]\n            [ M(a)_d^T   M_∞      ]\n     ```\n\n3. **Upper Bound Tour:** Use any algorithm that yields an upper bound, say T_UPPERBOUND = (t_1, t_2, ..., t_n), for an optimal tour in M(a).\n\n4. **Tour Transformation:** Replace T_UPPERBOUND by T = (t_1, t_{2+n}, t_2, t_{3+n}, t_3, ..., t_n, t_{1+n}) in M(s). By construction, |(t_{i+n}, t_i)| = 0, i = 1,2,...,n in M(s).\n\n5. **Cycle Decomposition:** It follows that the product of (t_{i+n}, t_i) equals σ_T where each 2-cycle (t_{i+n}, t_i) has a value of 0. We can always use a product of 2-cycles (edges) to obtain M(s)^(-1) - σ_T.\n\n6. **Acceptable Paths:** An acceptable path in M(s) consists alternately of non-zero and zero arcs. We cannot link acceptable cycles of the kind found in M(s) since by linking by deleting two arcs of form (t_{i+n}, t_i) or (t_i, t_{i+n}), we obtain a circuit containing two consecutive non-zero-valued directed edges.\n\n7. **Modified Floyd-Warshall Application:** Using the modified F-W algorithm, each cycle from a to b obtained in M(s) has a value no greater than any other cycle from a to b. Thus, using the modified F-W algorithm, there is only one way that we could obtain T_FWOPT of M(s): one minimal positively-valued acceptable cycle containing n arcs whose value is less than |T| or – if one can't be found, T_UPPERBOUND = T_FWOPT = T_OPT.\n\n8. **Complexity Claim:** The modified F-W algorithm when used for obtaining acceptable paths always obtains all acceptable paths in at most O(n⁴) running time. Since each such cycle is obtained using the modified F-W algorithm – together with an algorithm to ensure that an acceptable path obtained stays acceptable which requires backtracking in only a smaller number of cases than otherwise – we can obtain such a minimal positively-valued acceptable cycle containing n points of value less than |T| (if it exists) in polynomial time.\n\n   **Reasoning:** The Floyd-Warshall algorithm has O(n³) running time. Thus, even backtracking in every case, would raise the running time to at most O(n⁴).\n\n### III. The Construction of σ_T^(-1)(M(s))\n\nIn the original M(a), a_ii = ∞, i = 1,2,...,n.\n\n- In J-V M(a), a_ii = -M' where M' is the largest value of a nondiagonal entry in M(a)\n- In M(a)_d, a_ii = 0\n- M(a)_d is used in the construction of M(s)\n\nIn order that an optimal tour of M(s) yields an optimal tour of M(a), all arcs used in acceptable paths must belong to either M(a)_d or M(a)_d^T.\n\nBy applying σ_T^(-1) to the columns of M(s), we obtain a matrix whose diagonal elements all have the value zero, while all other entries have a positive value. This is because σ_T^(-1)(M(s)) = σ_T^(-1)(M(s))^(-).\n\nIt follows that all acceptable paths contain only positive values, implying that all acceptable cycles have positive values.\n\n---\n\n## Key Mathematical Objects",

[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]   "tool_use_result": {
[2026-04-23T17:03:38.056Z] [INFO]     "stdout": "# Original Paper: The Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem\n\n**Author:** Howard Kleiman\n**Year:** 2006 (submitted December 5, 2006; revised December 9, 2006)\n**arXiv ID:** [math.CO/0612114](https://arxiv.org/abs/math.CO/0612114)\n**Subject Classification:** General Mathematics (math.GM); MSC: 05-XX\n**Woeginger's List:** Entry #37\n\n---\n\n## Abstract\n\nThe paper proposes a polynomial-time algorithm for solving the Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP) by building on work by Jonker and Volgenannt. The approach transforms an n×n asymmetric cost matrix into a 2n×2n symmetric matrix with special properties, then applies a modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find an optimal tour.\n\nThe author acknowledges: \"If the proof of theorem 1 in section II is correct, since the asymmetric traveling salesman problem is NP-hard, P would equal NP.\"\n\n---\n\n## Paper Summary\n\n### I. Introduction\n\nStarting with M(a), an n×n asymmetric cost matrix, Jonker and Volgenannt [1] transformed it into a 2n × 2n symmetric cost matrix, M(s), where M(s) has unusual properties. One such property is that an optimal tour in M(s) yields an optimal tour in M(a).\n\nModifying M(s), the paper applies the modified Floyd-Warshall algorithm given in [2] to M(s). Let T be a tour that is an upper bound for an optimal tour in M(a). Due to the structure of M(s), the claim is that we either can always obtain an optimal tour in M(s) that is derived from only one minimal positively-valued cycle in σ_T^(-1)M whose value is less than |T| (i.e., we don't have to link circuits), or else T = T_OPT. Thus, the paper claims we can obtain an optimal tour in M(a) in at most polynomial running time.\n\n**Critical Statement:** \"If the proof of theorem 1 in section II is correct, since the asymmetric traveling salesman problem is NP-hard, P would equal NP.\"\n\n### II. A Theorem\n\n**Theorem 1.**\n\nLet M(a) be an n×n asymmetric cost matrix where a_ii = ∞, i = 1,2,...,n. Using a modified version of the symmetric cost matrix, M(s), obtained by Jonker and Volgenannt [1] as well as a result of Kleiman in [2] and the use of the modified F-W algorithm, we prove that we always can obtain an optimal solution to M(a) in polynomial time.\n\n**Proof Outline:**\n\n1. **Normalization:** If M(a) contains a non-positive entry, let m be the smallest value of all the entries in M(a). Add -m+1 to each entry of M(a) so that each entry now has a positive value.\n\n2. **Matrix Construction:**\n   - Let M_∞ be an n×n matrix each of whose entries is ∞\n   - Change each diagonal entry of M(a) into 0 to obtain the matrix M(a)_d\n   - Define M(a)_d^T as the transpose of M(a)_d\n   - Define the 2n × 2n symmetric matrix as:\n     ```\n     M(s) = [ M_∞        M(a)_d   ]\n            [ M(a)_d^T   M_∞      ]\n     ```\n\n3. **Upper Bound Tour:** Use any algorithm that yields an upper bound, say T_UPPERBOUND = (t_1, t_2, ..., t_n), for an optimal tour in M(a).\n\n4. **Tour Transformation:** Replace T_UPPERBOUND by T = (t_1, t_{2+n}, t_2, t_{3+n}, t_3, ..., t_n, t_{1+n}) in M(s). By construction, |(t_{i+n}, t_i)| = 0, i = 1,2,...,n in M(s).\n\n5. **Cycle Decomposition:** It follows that the product of (t_{i+n}, t_i) equals σ_T where each 2-cycle (t_{i+n}, t_i) has a value of 0. We can always use a product of 2-cycles (edges) to obtain M(s)^(-1) - σ_T.\n\n6. **Acceptable Paths:** An acceptable path in M(s) consists alternately of non-zero and zero arcs. We cannot link acceptable cycles of the kind found in M(s) since by linking by deleting two arcs of form (t_{i+n}, t_i) or (t_i, t_{i+n}), we obtain a circuit containing two consecutive non-zero-valued directed edges.\n\n7. **Modified Floyd-Warshall Application:** Using the modified F-W algorithm, each cycle from a to b obtained in M(s) has a value no greater than any other cycle from a to b. Thus, using the modified F-W algorithm, there is only one way that we could obtain T_FWOPT of M(s): one minimal positively-valued acceptable cycle containing n arcs whose value is less than |T| or – if one can't be found, T_UPPERBOUND = T_FWOPT = T_OPT.\n\n8. **Complexity Claim:** The modified F-W algorithm when used for obtaining acceptable paths always obtains all acceptable paths in at most O(n⁴) running time. Since each such cycle is obtained using the modified F-W algorithm – together with an algorithm to ensure that an acceptable path obtained stays acceptable which requires backtracking in only a smaller number of cases than otherwise – we can obtain such a minimal positively-valued acceptable cycle containing n points of value less than |T| (if it exists) in polynomial time.\n\n   **Reasoning:** The Floyd-Warshall algorithm has O(n³) running time. Thus, even backtracking in every case, would raise the running time to at most O(n⁴).\n\n### III. The Construction of σ_T^(-1)(M(s))\n\nIn the original M(a), a_ii = ∞, i = 1,2,...,n.\n\n- In J-V M(a), a_ii = -M' where M' is the largest value of a nondiagonal entry in M(a)\n- In M(a)_d, a_ii = 0\n- M(a)_d is used in the construction of M(s)\n\nIn order that an optimal tour of M(s) yields an optimal tour of M(a), all arcs used in acceptable paths must belong to either M(a)_d or M(a)_d^T.\n\nBy applying σ_T^(-1) to the columns of M(s), we obtain a matrix whose diagonal elements all have the value zero, while all other entries have a positive value. This is because σ_T^(-1)(M(s)) = σ_T^(-1)(M(s))^(-).\n\nIt follows that all acceptable paths contain only positive values, implying that all acceptable cycles have positive values.\n\n---\n\n## Key Mathematical Objects",

[2026-04-23T17:03:49.485Z] [INFO]       "content": "# A Trivial Solution to the PvNP Problem\n\n**Author**: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n**Date**: June 2008\n**Source**: [Academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/22390807/A_Trivial_Solution_to_the_PvNP_Problem)\n**Original URL** (may be offline): http://alixcomsi.com/18_A_trivial_solution_to_PvNP.pdf\n\n---\n\n## Note\n\nThis is a reconstruction of the paper's content based on the available abstract and references. The original PDF from Academia.edu requires login to download. The paper proposes a \"logical\" solution to P vs NP that the author admits is \"not significant computationally.\"\n\n---\n\n## Abstract\n\nThe paper argues that Gödel defined an arithmetical relation R(n) which can be \"constructively computed as true for any given natural number n, but is not Turing-computable as true for any given natural number n.\" The author claims this implies \"the current formulation of the PvNP problem admits a trivial logical solution that is not significant computationally,\" concluding that P ≠ NP.\n\n---\n\n## Main Argument\n\n### Gödelian Foundation\n\nThe paper builds on Gödel's incompleteness theorems, specifically focusing on:\n\n1. Gödel's construction of arithmetical propositions that are true but not provable within a formal system\n2. The distinction between truth and provability in formal systems\n3. The existence of propositions that can be verified for specific instances but not decided algorithmically in general\n\n### Constructive vs Turing Computability\n\nThe author distinguishes between two notions:\n\n**Constructive Computability:**\n- A relation R(n) is constructively computable if we can verify its truth for any given specific natural number n\n- This corresponds to checking individual instances\n- Related to verification or proof-checking\n\n**Turing Computability:**\n- A relation is Turing-computable if there exists a general algorithm that can compute its truth value for all natural numbers\n- This corresponds to having a complete decision procedure\n- Related to algorithmic decidability\n\n### The Core Claim\n\nThe paper claims:\n\n1. Gödel's work demonstrates an arithmetical relation R(n) that is:\n   - Constructively computable (can verify truth for each n)\n   - NOT Turing-computable (no general algorithm exists)\n\n2. This separation mirrors the P vs NP distinction:\n   - NP: problems where solutions can be verified efficiently\n   - P: problems that can be solved (decided) efficiently\n\n3. Therefore, by analogy to Gödel's result, P ≠ NP\n\n### The \"Trivial\" Qualification",

[2026-04-23T17:03:53.286Z] [INFO]       "content": "# Bhupinder Singh Anand (2008) - P≠NP Attempt\n\n**Navigation:** [↑ Back to Repository Root](../../../README.md) | [Woeginger's List Entry #44](https://wscor.win.tue.nl/woeginger/P-versus-NP.htm)\n\n---\n\n## Metadata\n\n- **Attempt ID**: 44 (from Woeginger's list)\n- **Author**: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n- **Year**: June 2008\n- **Claim**: P ≠ NP\n- **Paper Title**: \"A trivial solution to the PvNP problem\"\n- **Source**: [Academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/22390807/A_Trivial_Solution_to_the_PvNP_Problem)\n\n---\n\n## Quick Links\n\n- **[Original Paper (ORIGINAL.md)](./ORIGINAL.md)** - Reconstruction of the paper's content\n- **[Proof Attempt (proof/)](./proof/)** - Formalization of Anand's argument\n- **[Refutation (refutation/)](./refutation/)** - Why the proof fails\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\nBhupinder Singh Anand's 2008 paper claims to prove P ≠ NP by distinguishing between **constructive computability** (verifiable truth) and **Turing computability** (algorithmically computable truth). The author argues that Gödel's construction of undecidable propositions demonstrates this distinction, implying P ≠ NP holds in a \"formal logical sense\" (though admittedly \"not significant computationally\").\n\n### The Main Claim\n\n> \"Gödel defined an arithmetical tautology R(n) which can be constructively computed as true for any given natural number n, but is not Turing-computable as true for any given natural number n. This implies that the current formulation of the PvNP problem admits a trivial logical solution that is not significant computationally.\"\n\n---\n\n## The Argument Structure\n\n1. **Gödelian Foundation**: Gödel's incompleteness shows some relations can be verified for specific instances but not decided algorithmically\n2. **Verification vs Decision**: This demonstrates \"constructive computability\" ≠ \"Turing computability\"\n3. **Analogy to P vs NP**: This distinction mirrors NP (verification) vs P (decision)\n4. **Conclusion**: Therefore P ≠ NP in a \"formal logical sense\"\n5. **Caveat**: The author admits this is \"trivial\" and \"not significant computationally\"\n\n---\n\n## The Fatal Error: Category Confusion\n\n### Undecidability ≠ Complexity\n\nThe fundamental error is **conflating computability theory with complexity theory**:",

[2026-04-23T17:03:53.349Z] [INFO]         "content": "1\t# Forward Proof Formalization: Anand 2008\n2\t\n3\tThis directory contains the formal proof attempt following Anand's approach as faithfully as possible.\n4\t\n5\t## Contents\n6\t\n7\t- `lean/AnandProofAttempt.lean` - Lean 4 formalization\n8\t- `rocq/AnandProofAttempt.v` - Rocq formalization\n9\t\n10\t## What These Formalizations Capture\n11\t\n12\tThe formalizations attempt to capture:\n13\t\n14\t1. **Gödel's Undecidability Results**: The construction of arithmetical relations that are \"constructively computable\" but \"not Turing-computable\"\n15\t2. **Distinction Between Verification and Decision**: Anand's claim about constructive computability vs algorithmic computability\n16\t3. **The Logical Separation Argument**: The attempt to derive P ≠ NP from logical/Gödelian principles\n17\t4. **The \"Trivial Solution\" Claim**: Anand's assertion that P ≠ NP holds in a \"formal logical sense\"\n18\t\n19\t## The Attempted Proof Logic\n20\t\n21\tAnand's argument proceeds:\n22\t\n23\t1. **Gödelian Foundation**: Gödel showed there exist arithmetical relations R(n) that can be \"constructively computed as true for any given n\" but cannot be \"Turing-computed\" in general\n24\t2. **Verification vs Decision**: This demonstrates a fundamental distinction between verification (checking specific instances) and decision (algorithmic solution)\n25\t3. **Analogy to P vs NP**: This distinction is analogous to the P vs NP question\n26\t4. **Logical Conclusion**: Therefore, P ≠ NP holds in a \"formal logical sense\"\n27\t5. **Computational Insignificance**: The author admits this is \"trivial\" and \"not significant computationally\"\n28\t\n29\t## Where the Formalizations Stop\n30\t\n31\tThe formalizations include `sorry` (Lean) and `Admitted` (Rocq) placeholders at the critical gaps where Anand's argument fails:\n32\t\n33\t1. **Category Confusion**: The leap from undecidability (computability theory) to complexity (time bounds) cannot be formalized\n34\t2. **Missing Time Analysis**: No connection to polynomial time vs exponential time\n35\t3. **Non-Sequitur**: The logical premises don't imply the complexity-theoretic conclusion\n36\t4. **\"Trivial\" Admission**: The solution lacks computational content by the author's own admission\n37\t\n38\t## Implementation Note\n39\t\n40\tSince Anand's paper doesn't provide step-by-step mathematical derivations, these formalizations capture the high-level argument structure and mark with `sorry`/`Admitted` where the invalid inferential leaps occur.\n41\t\n42\t## See Also\n43\t\n44\t- [`../README.md`](../README.md) - Overview of the attempt\n45\t- [`../ORIGINAL.md`](../ORIGINAL.md) - Markdown conversion of the [2026-04-23T17:03:53.349Z] [INFO]       "content": "# Forward Proof Formalization: Anand 2008\n\nThis directory contains the formal proof attempt following Anand's approach as faithfully as possible.\n\n## Contents\n\n- `lean/AnandProofAttempt.lean` - Lean 4 formalization\n- `rocq/AnandProofAttempt.v` - Rocq formalization\n\n## What These Formalizations Capture\n\nThe formalizations attempt to capture:\n\n1. **Gödel's Undecidability Results**: The construction of arithmetical relations that are \"constructively computable\" but \"not Turing-computable\"\n2. **Distinction Between Verification and Decision**: Anand's claim about constructive computability vs algorithmic computability\n3. **The Logical Separation Argument**: The attempt to derive P ≠ NP from logical/Gödelian principles\n4. **The \"Trivial Solution\" Claim**: Anand's assertion that P ≠ NP holds in a \"formal logical sense\"\n\n## The Attempted Proof Logic\n\nAnand's argument proceeds:\n\n1. **Gödelian Foundation**: Gödel showed there exist arithmetical relations R(n) that can be \"constructively computed as true for any given n\" but cannot be \"Turing-computed\" in general\n2. **Verification vs Decision**: This demonstrates a fundamental distinction between verification (checking specific instances) and decision (algorithmic solution)\n3. **Analogy to P vs NP**: This distinction is analogous to the P vs NP question\n4. **Logical Conclusion**: Therefore, P ≠ NP holds in a \"formal logical sense\"\n5. **Computational Insignificance**: The author admits this is \"trivial\" and \"not significant computationally\"\n\n## Where the Formalizations Stop\n\nThe formalizations include `sorry` (Lean) and `Admitted` (Rocq) placeholders at the critical gaps where Anand's argument fails:\n\n1. **Category Confusion**: The leap from undecidability (computability theory) to complexity (time bounds) cannot be formalized\n2. **Missing Time Analysis**: No connection to polynomial time vs exponential time\n3. **Non-Sequitur**: The logical premises don't imply the complexity-theoretic conclusion\n4. **\"Trivial\" Admission**: The solution lacks computational content by the author's own admission\n\n## Implementation Note\n\nSince Anand's paper doesn't provide step-by-step mathematical derivations, these formalizations capture the high-level argument structure and mark with `sorry`/`Admitted` where the invalid inferential leaps occur.\n\n## See Also\n\n- [`../README.md`](../README.md) - Overview of the attempt\n- [`../ORIGINAL.md`](../ORIGINAL.md) - Markdown conversion of the original paper\n- [`../refutation/README.md`](../refutation/README.md) - Detailed explanation of why the proof fails\n",

[2026-04-23T17:04:15.563Z] [INFO]         "content": "1\t/-\n2\t  Anand's 2008 Proof Attempt: P ≠ NP via Gödelian Arguments\n3\t\n4\t  Paper: \"A trivial solution to the PvNP problem\" (June 2008)\n5\t  Author: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n6\t\n7\t  This formalization attempts to capture Anand's argument as presented,\n8\t  marking with `sorry` the places where the logic breaks down.\n9\t-/\n10\t\n11\tnamespace AnandProofAttempt\n12\t\n13\t/-! ## Basic Complexity Definitions -/\n14\t\n15\tdef Language := Nat → Prop\n16\tdef TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n17\t\n18\tdef PolynomialTime (f : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n19\t  ∃ c k : Nat, ∀ n : Nat, f n ≤ c * (n ^ k) + c\n20\t\n21\tdef InP (L : Language) : Prop :=\n22\t  ∃ (M : Nat → Bool) (t : TimeComplexity),\n23\t    PolynomialTime t ∧\n24\t    ∀ x : Nat, L x ↔ M x = true\n25\t\n26\tdef InNP (L : Language) : Prop :=\n27\t  ∃ (V : Nat → Nat → Bool) (t : TimeComplexity),\n28\t    PolynomialTime t ∧\n29\t    ∀ x : Nat, L x ↔ ∃ c : Nat, V x c = true\n30\t\n31\t/-! ## Anand's Gödelian Framework -/\n32\t\n33\t-- Formal provability in a logical system\n34\tdef FormallyProvable (statement : Prop) : Prop :=\n35\t  True  -- Simplified placeholder\n36\t\n37\t-- Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem\n38\taxiom goedel_incompleteness :\n39\t  ∃ statement : Prop, statement ∧ ¬ FormallyProvable statement\n40\t\n41\t-- Anand's notion of \"constructive computability\"\n42\t-- In the paper, this means we can verify truth for specific instances\n43\tdef ConstructivelyComputable (R : Nat → Prop) : Prop :=\n44\t  ∀ n : Nat, ∃ (procedure : Nat → Bool), R n ↔ procedure n = true\n45\t\n46\t-- Turing computability (general algorithmic decidability)\n47\tdef TuringComputable (R : Nat → Prop) : Prop :=\n48\t  ∃ (M : Nat → Bool), ∀ n : Nat, R n ↔ M n = true\n49\t\n50\t/-! ## Anand's Core Claim -/\n51\t\n52\t-- CLAIM 1: Gödel's construction gives us an R that is\n53\t-- constructively computable but not Turing computable\n54\t-- From paper: \"Gödel defined an arithmetical tautology R(n) which can be\n55\t-- constructively computed as true for any given natural number n, but\n56\t-- is not Turing-computable as true for any given natural number n\"\n57\t\n58\taxiom anand_goedel_relation :\n59\t  ∃ R : Nat → Prop,\n60\t    ConstructivelyComputable R ∧\n61\t    ¬ TuringComputable R\n62\t\n63\t/-! ## The Attempted Proof -/\n64\t\n65\t-- CLAIM 2: This distinction between constructive and Turing computability\n66\t-- is analogous to the P vs NP distinction\n67\t--\n68\t-- NOTE: This is where the argument makes an invalid leap!\n69\t-- Constructive computability (with no time bound) ≠ Polynomial-time verification\n70\t-- Turing computability (decidability) ≠ Polynomial-time decision\n71\t\n72\taxiom anand_analogy_claim :\n73\t  (∃ R, ConstructivelyComputable R ∧ ¬ TuringComputable R) →\n74\t  (∃ L, InNP L ∧ ¬ InP L)\n75\t  -- ❌ This axiom represents an INVALID inference\n76\t  -- The antecedent is about computability (no time bounds)\n77\t  -- The consequent is about complexity (polynomial time bounds)\n78\t\n79\t-- CLAIM 3: Therefore P ≠ NP\n80\t-- From paper: \"This implies that the current formulation of the PvNP problem\n81\t-- admits a trivial logical solution\"\n82\t\n83\ttheorem anand_p_neq_np : ¬ (∀ L, InP L ↔ InNP L) := by\n84\t  -- Step 1: Invoke Gödel's relation\n85\t  have h_goedel := anand_goedel_relation\n86\t\n87\t  -- Step 2: Apply the (invalid) analogy\n88\t  have h_sep := anand_analogy_claim h_goedel\n89\t\n90\t  -- Step 3: Conclude P ≠ NP\n91\t  intro h_eq\n92\t  obtain ⟨L, h_np, h_not_p⟩ := h_sep\n93\t  have h_p : InP L := by\n94\t    have h_iff := h_eq L\n95\t    exact h_iff.mpr h_np\n96\t  contradiction\n97\t\n98\t/-\n99\t  NOTE: This proof \"succeeds\" only because we assumed anand_analogy_claim as an axiom.\n100\t  But anand_analogy_claim is INVALID - it conflates:\n101\t  - Computability theory (undecidability) with Complexity theory (time bounds)\n102\t  - Constructive verification (arbitrary time) with NP (polynomial time)\n103\t  - Turing decidability with P (polynomial time decision)\n104\t\n105\t  The paper provides NO JUSTIFICATION for this axiom.\n106\t-/\n107\t\n108\t/-! ## The Author's Own Caveat -/\n109\t\n110\t-- From the paper: The solution is \"not significant computationally\"\n111\t-- This admission reveals that the argument doesn't address computational complexity\n112\t\n113\taxiom anand_not_computational :\n114\t  -- The \"proof\" above lacks computational significance\n115\t  True\n116\t\n117\t/-\n118\t  SUMMARY OF THE PROOF ATTEMPT:\n119\t\n120\t  1. ✓ Gödel showed some relations are constructively verifiable but not Turing-decidable\n121\t  2. ✗ INVALID LEAP: This is analogous to NP vs P\n122\t  3. ✗ Therefore P ≠ NP\n123\t\n124\t  The proof fails because:\n125\t  - Step 2 conflates different hierarchies (computability vs complexity)\n126\t  - No analysis of TIME COMPLEXITY is provided\n127\t  - The author admits it's \"not significant computationally\"\n128\t  - Missing: lower bounds, polynomial vs exponential analysis\n129\t-/\n130\t\n131\tend AnandProofAttempt\n132\t"

[2026-04-23T17:04:15.563Z] [INFO]       "content": "/-\n  Anand's 2008 Proof Attempt: P ≠ NP via Gödelian Arguments\n\n  Paper: \"A trivial solution to the PvNP problem\" (June 2008)\n  Author: Bhupinder Singh Anand\n\n  This formalization attempts to capture Anand's argument as presented,\n  marking with `sorry` the places where the logic breaks down.\n-/\n\nnamespace AnandProofAttempt\n\n/-! ## Basic Complexity Definitions -/\n\ndef Language := Nat → Prop\ndef TimeComplexity := Nat → Nat\n\ndef PolynomialTime (f : TimeComplexity) : Prop :=\n  ∃ c k : Nat, ∀ n : Nat, f n ≤ c * (n ^ k) + c\n\ndef InP (L : Language) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (M : Nat → Bool) (t : TimeComplexity),\n    PolynomialTime t ∧\n    ∀ x : Nat, L x ↔ M x = true\n\ndef InNP (L : Language) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (V : Nat → Nat → Bool) (t : TimeComplexity),\n    PolynomialTime t ∧\n    ∀ x : Nat, L x ↔ ∃ c : Nat, V x c = true\n\n/-! ## Anand's Gödelian Framework -/\n\n-- Formal provability in a logical system\ndef FormallyProvable (statement : Prop) : Prop :=\n  True  -- Simplified placeholder\n\n-- Gödel's First Incompleteness Theorem\naxiom goedel_incompleteness :\n  ∃ statement : Prop, statement ∧ ¬ FormallyProvable statement\n\n-- Anand's notion of \"constructive computability\"\n-- In the paper, this means we can verify truth for specific instances\ndef ConstructivelyComputable (R : Nat → Prop) : Prop :=\n  ∀ n : Nat, ∃ (procedure : Nat → Bool), R n ↔ procedure n = true\n\n-- Turing computability (general algorithmic decidability)\ndef TuringComputable (R : Nat → Prop) : Prop :=\n  ∃ (M : Nat → Bool), ∀ n : Nat, R n ↔ M n = true\n\n/-! ## Anand's Core Claim -/\n\n-- CLAIM 1: Gödel's construction gives us an R that is\n-- constructively computable but not Turing computable\n-- From paper: \"Gödel defined an arithmetical tautology R(n) which can be\n-- constructively computed as true for any given natural number n, but\n-- is not Turing-computable as true for any given natural number n\"\n\naxiom anand_goedel_relation :\n  ∃ R : Nat → Prop,\n    ConstructivelyComputable R ∧\n    ¬ TuringComputable R\n\n/-! ## The Attempted Proof -/\n\n-- CLAIM 2: This distinction between constructive and Turing computability\n-- is analogous to the P vs NP distinction\n--\n-- NOTE: This is where the argument makes an invalid leap!\n-- Constructive computability (with no time bound) ≠ Polynomial-time verification\n-- Turing computability (decidability) ≠ Polynomial-time decision\n\naxiom anand_analogy_claim :\n  (∃ R, ConstructivelyComputable R ∧ ¬ TuringComputable R) →\n  (∃ L, InNP L ∧ ¬ InP L)\n  -- ❌ This axiom represents an INVALID inference\n  -- The antecedent is about computability (no time bounds)\n  -- The consequent is about complexity (polynomial time bounds)\n\n-- CLAIM 3: Therefore P ≠ NP\n-- From paper: \"This implies that the current formulation of the PvNP problem\n-- admits a trivial logical solution\"\n\ntheorem anand_p_neq_np : ¬ (∀ L, InP L ↔ InNP L) := by\n  -- Step 1: Invoke Gödel's relation\n  have h_goedel := anand_goedel_relation\n\n  -- Step 2: Apply the (invalid) analogy\n  have h_sep := anand_analogy_claim h_goedel\n\n  -- Step 3: Conclude P ≠ NP\n  intro h_eq\n  obtain ⟨L, h_np, h_not_p⟩ := h_sep\n  have h_p : InP L := by\n    have h_iff := h_eq L\n    exact h_iff.mpr h_np\n  contradiction\n\n/-\n  NOTE: This proof \"succeeds\" only because we assumed anand_analogy_claim as an axiom.\n  But anand_analogy_claim is INVALID - it conflates:\n  - Computability theory (undecidability) with Complexity theory (time bounds)\n  - Constructive verification (arbitrary time) with NP (polynomial time)\n  - Turing decidability with P (polynomial time decision)\n\n  The paper provides NO JUSTIFICATION for this axiom.\n-/\n\n/-! ## The Author's Own Caveat -/\n\n-- From the paper: The solution is \"not significant computationally\"\n-- This admission reveals that the argument doesn't address computational complexity\n\naxiom anand_not_computational :\n  -- The \"proof\" above lacks computational significance\n  True\n\n/-\n  SUMMARY OF THE PROOF ATTEMPT:\n\n  1. ✓ Gödel showed some relations are constructively verifiable but not Turing-decidable\n  2. ✗ INVALID LEAP: This is analogous to NP vs P\n  3. ✗ Therefore P ≠ NP\n\n  The proof fails because:\n  - Step 2 conflates different hierarchies (computability vs complexity)\n  - No analysis of TIME COMPLEXITY is provided\n  - The author admits it's \"not significant computationally\"\n  - Missing: lower bounds, polynomial vs exponential analysis\n-/\n\nend AnandProofAttempt\n",
